feed.title: Shrividya Hegde
feed.subtitle: Senior AI Data Engineer with 10+ years of experience in data engineering, AI/ML, Apache Airflow, and test automation. Airflow Champion, OSS contributor, building LLM & RAG pipelines. NJ Women in Data Chapter Lead.

entry.title: Getting Started with Apache Airflow: A Practical Guide

link.title: Getting Started with Apache Airflow: A Practical Guide

entry.content:
Apache Airflow is the industry standard for orchestrating data pipelines. Think of it like a traffic controller for your data — it decides what runs, when it runs, and what happens if something goes wrong.

What is a DAG?

A DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a pipeline where tasks run in a defined order, without loops. Like a recipe — you can’t frost a cake before baking it.

from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

def extract():
    print("Extracting data...")

def transform():
    print("Transforming data...")

with DAG(
    dag_id="my_first_dag",
    start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1),
    schedule="@daily",
    catchup=False,
) as dag:

    extract_task = PythonOperator(
        task_id="extract",
        python_callable=extract,
    )

    transform_task = PythonOperator(
        task_id="transform",
        python_callable=transform,
    )

    extract_task >> transform_task

Key Concepts

Tasks are the individual steps — like ingredients in your recipe.

Operators are the task types — Python, Bash, SQL, HTTP, and hundreds more.

Connections store credentials securely so your DAGs can talk to databases and APIs.

Best Practices

  1. Keep tasks small and focused — one task, one job
  2. Use catchup=False unless you specifically need backfills
  3. Store secrets in Airflow Connections, never hardcode them
  4. Always set retries for production DAGs

Want to go deeper? Reach out on LinkedIn.
entry.summary: A hands-on walkthrough of DAG authoring, scheduling, and best practices for production Airflow pipelines.
